_meta.purpose: Static reference for the 'What's allowed on the plot' planning card — concrete renovation/extension possibilities for Alex's plot, in plain English. NO numbers are fabricated as plot-specific; everything here is either (a) a documented plot FACT, or (b) a GENERAL planning rule explicitly labelled generic. The plot-specific building figures (ground coverage, built-area ratio, height, boundary setbacks) live in his applicable plan and are surfaced live by planning.js from the planning registry where the GIS returns them.
_meta.honesty: The plot data (lot / area / authority) is fictional, for demo purposes only. Every item carries a 'scope' of either 'plot' (a verified fact about this lot), 'generic' (a general planning rule that applies unless his specific applicable plan says otherwise), or 'plan' (a number that exists only inside the governing plan document — read it via the plans-archive link).
_meta.compiledNote: General rules reflect generic regional planning regulations and a local permit-exemption scheme as of compile date; rules and the plot's plans can change — always confirm at the local planning authority before building.

plot.committeeNote: Larkmont falls within the planning jurisdiction of the Larkmont local authority — that's where you submit a permit application and verify the exact building rights.
plot.note: Plot data is entirely fictional (demo) — the lot/area figures are not real.

intro.he: What you can build or add on the plot — a building extension, a reinforced safe room, an extra floor, and yard construction. The exact figures (how much coverage, what height, distance from the boundary) are set by the building plan that applies to the plot; here we explain what is allowed in principle and how to read the real number from the plan.

possibilities.title: Building extension / expansion
possibilities.lead: Enlarging the house — on the ground floor or an upper floor — up to the limit of the plot's building rights.

points.he: Every plot is defined by a ground-coverage percentage (the share of the land that may be built on — e.g. 30%–40% of 500 m²) and by a built-area ratio (the total built m² allowed across all floors).
The gap between what is built today and what the building plan allows = the expansion potential. Adding beyond that requires a variance or an amendment to the building plan.

The expansion must respect the building setbacks from the boundary — the minimum distance allowed from each plot boundary (front/side/rear). Building closer to the boundary requires a variance and sometimes a neighbor's consent.

readNumber.he: How to read the coverage percentage from the building plan: open the plan in the local plans archive (the link next to each plan above) → the building-rights table / construction provisions → the 'ground coverage' and 'permitted built area' rows. That's where the plot's exact number is.

estimateNote.he: Illustrative only (not an official number): if the building plan allows 35% coverage on a 500 m² plot, that's about 175 m² of permitted footprint per floor. The real number — only from the building plan.

possibilities.title: Reinforced safe room — adding or upgrading
possibilities.lead: A reinforced safe room for the house — adding one to an existing house, or upgrading/enlarging an existing safe room.

points.he: A reinforced safe room is built to the safe-room standard (concrete walls and ceiling, reinforced door and window, ventilation). It is part of the permit application.
It usually qualifies for a building-rights concession: the safe room's area (up to ~9 m² net) is exempt or only partly counted, so building it usually doesn't 'eat into' the regular expansion rights.
If the existing house has no safe room — that's usually the first addition worth folding into any substantial renovation.

readNumber.he: The safe-room standard publishes the current dimensions and requirements; the local planning authority approves the location relative to the building setbacks.

possibilities.title: Adding a floor
possibilities.lead: Adding a floor above the existing structure — subject to the height and the number of floors the building plan permits.

points.he: The building plan sets the permitted number of floors and a maximum height (in meters, or a number of levels). If the house is lower than allowed — there may be room for an additional floor.
Adding a floor also depends on the existing structure's ability to carry it (a structural engineer's assessment) — not only on the rights.
Even when adding a floor you must stay within the overall built-area ratio and the building setbacks.

readNumber.he: In the building plan: the 'maximum number of floors' and 'maximum building height' rows. Compare them with the existing state to see whether any 'spare height' remains.

possibilities.title: Yard construction
possibilities.lead: A pergola, a shed, and an independent dwelling unit (a housing unit / 'second apartment') in the yard.

points.he: Pergola — of limited size and subject to the exemption conditions (see 'permit exemption' below) — usually without a permit.
Shed — a light storage structure up to a limited area/height is usually exempt from a permit; beyond that a permit is required.

Additional dwelling unit (apartment split) — splitting a private house into an independent unit is possible under the dwelling-unit split provisions (adding housing units), subject to the local building plan's conditions, parking and infrastructure. Requires a permit.

readNumber.he: Splitting into an additional dwelling unit is conditional on the local planning authority's approval and on the building plan not prohibiting it — check with them before planning.

exemptions.title: What doesn't require a permit — the exemption list (local exemption rules)

intro.he: The local permit-exemption rules allow a series of small works without a building permit, provided you stay within the size limits and meet the safety requirements, and notify the local authority as required. Exemption from a permit is not exemption from professional responsibility or from compliance with the building plan.

items.he: Pergola — up to limited area and height, open at its top.
Shed / light portable structure — up to limited area and height.
Internal changes that don't touch structural elements (moving a drywall partition, renovating a kitchen/bathroom without affecting stability).
Solar system / solar collectors / solar water heater on the roof.
Fence / retaining wall up to a limited height, and a paved surface low to the ground.
Awning / carport canopy (lightweight and installed per the conditions), and light gardening/shade structures.

caveat.he: The exact numeric limits (how many m², what height, distance from the boundary) appear in the exemption rules themselves and apply subject to the building plan in force for the plot. Even work that is exempt from a permit must comply with the building setbacks and the plan's provisions.

footer.he: The general rules hold at the regional level; the plot's exact figures (coverage percentage, height, building setbacks, built-area ratio) are set by the building plan and by the decisions of the Larkmont local planning authority. Before any planning — verify with them.
